Name: adaptive_bit_allocation
Title: Adaptive Bit Allocation for Model Compression via Rate-Distortion Analysis
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging a dynamic approach to bit allocation based on task-relevant distortion metrics, we can significantly enhance model compression without sacrificing predictive performance, outperforming static allocation methods.
Related Work: Existing methods like SQR and RDP frameworks focus on static profiles or specific task adaptations. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a real-time adaptive bit allocation mechanism grounded in rate-distortion theory, which dynamically adjusts based on the observed task performance and input distributions.
Abstract: In the realm of model compression, effective bit allocation is crucial for maintaining performance while minimizing resource usage. We propose a novel adaptive bit allocation framework that employs real-time rate-distortion analysis to optimize the distribution of bits across model parameters according to their task relevance. The framework operates by continuously evaluating the trade-off between model complexity and predictive accuracy, leveraging metrics such as mutual information and conditional entropy to guide the allocation process. This approach aims to minimize the task-relevant distortion while adhering to a predetermined bit-rate constraint. We will demonstrate the efficacy of our method through a series of experiments on various deep learning architectures, measuring performance against traditional static allocation methods. The proposed method not only aims to achieve superior compression ratios but also seeks to provide a more interpretable understanding of the relationship between model architecture, bit allocation, and performance metrics.
Experiments: Implement the adaptive bit allocation framework on a selected neural network architecture and establish a baseline with static bit allocation.
Evaluate performance across multiple datasets and tasks, measuring metrics such as accuracy, compression ratio, and task-relevant distortion.
Analyze the impact of dynamic adjustments in bit allocation on model performance during inference under varying input distributions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method relies on accurate estimation of task-relevant distortion, which may be challenging in practice. Additionally, the computational overhead of real-time analysis could limit applicability in extremely resource-constrained environments.
